original_reasoning: Ar halqa=ring/circle/remaining liquid in vessel; Lat alcohol=kohl/refined substance (via Arabic al-kuhl); shared Arabic origin — Arabic halqa and kuhl both involve refined circular/pure things, clear connection via Arabic derivation pathway
Ar difda'=frog; Lat paludifer=marsh-making; frog is the canonical marsh-dwelling creature — ecological semantic connection
نقر=to strike/peck/pursue; insequor=follow/pursue/attack; both involve striking pursuit
أراك=toothbrush-tree (Salvadora persica); larix=larch tree; both are specific named trees
تهبرس=swaggering strut; colobathra=stilts; elevated swaggering gait connection
بأبأ=to buzz/say baba (صوت); bombio=to buzz — onomatopoeic sound match

Arabic الحلكة from حلك = intense blackness, dark coloring. Latin/Medieval Latin alcohol derives from Arabic الكُحْل (al-kuhl) = kohl, the black antimony eye cosmetic. The connection is through the blackness domain: حلكة (blackness) relates to كحل (black eye cosmetic) as descriptive quality to the substance itself. Not a direct cognate but a shared Semitic color-substance chain. The calibration example الحلقه→alcohol = 0.60 applies similarly here.

Arabic نقر (root n-q-r) has rich polysemy: (1) to peck, tap, strike, (2) نَقَّرَ = to investigate, examine, scrutinize (Form II), (3) نقر الرجل = to examine/criticize a person, (4) to dig/hollow out. Latin anquiro (an- + quaero) = to investigate judicially, inquire into, examine carefully. The semantic match on meaning (2-3) is strong: both describe careful, detailed examination/scrutiny of someone or something. The phonetic chain n-q-r → -nquir- shows consonant preservation (n-q-r → n-qu-r). The Latin prefix an- could mask the root. This is a genuine find Sonnet underscored.
